root.narration: You just crushed a ball into the gap in left-center field! You're sprinting around first base and heading to second for a double! You slide into second base, but your momentum carries you! You slid a few feet PAST the base and you're lying in the dirt behind second base. Your hand isn't touching the bag! The center fielder is picking up the ball. The shortstop is right next to you at second base. What do you do RIGHT NOW?

choices.text: Get back on the base NOW! Reach back and grab the bag before the shortstop tags you!
Stand up, dust yourself off, and casually walk back to the base.
Just lay there. You already slid into second so you must be safe, right?

outcome-scramble-great.narration: You instantly realize you're off the bag and LUNGE back toward second base! Your hand slaps the base just as the shortstop reaches down to tag you. SAFE! The umpire saw it clearly -- your hand was on the bag when the tag arrived. Your heart is pounding, but you're safe with a double! That was close! You almost got tagged out on a play where you should have been safe easily.

outcome.headline: Quick Hands! Safe at Second!
outcome.explanation: Great awareness! When you slide past a base, you are NOT safe until you're touching the bag. Unlike first base, where you can overrun the bag safely, every other base requires you to STAY on the bag. If a fielder tags you while you're not touching the base, you're out! The moment you felt yourself sliding past, you reached back immediately. That quick reaction saved you. In the future, try to 'hook slide' -- curling your body so your foot catches the corner of the base. This prevents you from sliding past it.
outcome.whatToRemember: If you slide past a base, reach back and touch it IMMEDIATELY! You're not safe until you're touching the bag. Only first base can be overrun safely.

keyTerms.definition: When your slide carries you past the base and you're no longer touching it. This is dangerous because the defense can tag you out while you're off the bag!
A sliding technique where you curl your body to the side and hook the base with your foot or hand. This prevents you from sliding past the base.
First base is the ONLY base you can run past safely without being tagged out (as long as you turn right toward foul territory). At every other base, you must stay on the bag!

outcome-casual-bad.narration: You slowly stand up, brush the dirt off your pants, and start walking back toward second base. The shortstop sees their chance -- the center fielder throws the ball in and the shortstop catches it. They reach over and tag you while you're still a step away from the base. OUT! The umpire says you were not on the base when the tag was applied. Your nice double just turned into an embarrassing out because you weren't paying attention!

outcome.headline: Tagged Out! Too Casual!
outcome.explanation: This is a baserunning mistake that happens because of a misunderstanding of the rules. You are NEVER safe just because you slid into the base earlier. You have to be TOUCHING the base to be safe. The moment you stood up and walked away, you were fair game for a tag. The shortstop was smart -- they called for the ball and tagged you while you were casually strolling back. In baseball, the play isn't over until the umpire says it's over. Always stay on the base and stay alert! Smart defenders are always looking for runners who drift off the bag.
outcome.whatToRemember: Stay on the base and stay alert! You're only safe while touching the bag. Don't casually walk away -- defenders are ALWAYS watching for runners off the base.

outcome-lay-bad.narration: You lay in the dirt behind second base, thinking you're safe because you slid into the base. But your body is three feet past the bag! The center fielder throws the ball to the shortstop, who walks over and tags you while you're lying there. OUT! You look up at the umpire, confused. 'You weren't on the base,' the ump explains. Your double just became an out because you didn't realize you had to STAY on the base.

outcome.headline: Tagged Out! Not on the Base!
outcome.explanation: Just because you touched the base during your slide doesn't mean you're safe forever. You have to MAINTAIN contact with the base. Think of it like a game of tag -- 'base' is only safe as long as you're touching it! When you slid past the bag, you broke contact. At that point, you were like any other runner who's not on a base -- tag-able. This is a really important rule to understand: at second base and third base, you must STAY on the bag. The only exception is first base, where you're allowed to overrun the bag as long as you turn toward foul territory.
outcome.whatToRemember: You must be TOUCHING the base to be safe. Sliding into it and then sliding past it means you're off the base and can be tagged out!
